explanation:
JavaScript has three ways to declare variables: `var` (function-scoped, hoisted, avoid), `let` (block-scoped, mutable), and `const` (block-scoped, binding immutable). The type system is dynamic â€” variables can hold any type â€” but quirky coercion rules make `"5" + 3` equal `"53"` while `"5" - 3` equals `2`. Understanding these rules prevents subtle bugs.

JavaScript has seven primitive types (string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, symbol, null) and one object type. Primitives are immutable and compared by value; objects are mutable and compared by reference. The `typeof` operator identifies types, but `typeof null === "object"` is a famous 30-year-old bug.
key_takeaway: Always use `const` by default, `let` when reassignment is needed, never `var`. Always use `===` for comparisons to avoid coercion surprises.
try_it: Predict the output of `[] + []`, `[] + {}`, `{} + []`, and `!!""]` before running them. Then explain each result using coercion rules.

explanation:
Functions in JavaScript are first-class values â€” they can be stored in variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions. Three syntax styles exist: function declarations (hoisted), function expressions (not hoisted), and arrow functions (lexical `this`, no own `arguments`). Arrow functions are concise but intentionally limited â€” they're not suitable for object methods or constructors.

Scope determines variable visibility. `var` is function-scoped; `let`/`const` are block-scoped. Closures let inner functions access outer function variables even after the outer function returns â€” this is how callbacks, event handlers, and module patterns work in JavaScript.
key_takeaway: Closures capture variables by reference â€” `let` in loops creates a new binding per iteration, while `var` shares one binding (the classic loop bug).
try_it: Write a `createAccumulator()` function that returns an object with `add(n)`, `reset()`, and `total` getter. Use closures to maintain state.

explanation:
Arrays are ordered collections with powerful built-in methods for transformation, filtering, and aggregation. They're actually objects with numeric keys â€” `typeof [] === "object"`. The array methods `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, `find`, `some`, and `every` are the functional programming toolkit that replaces most `for` loops in modern JavaScript.

Objects are key-value stores where keys are strings (or Symbols). They support prototypal inheritance â€” when you access a property, JavaScript looks up the prototype chain if the object doesn't have it directly. The spread operator (`...`) and `Object.assign` enable shallow copying and merging.
key_takeaway: Array methods (`map`, `filter`, `reduce`) compose into data pipelines â€” chain them to transform collections declaratively instead of writing imperative loops.
try_it: Given an array of transactions `{type: "credit"|"debit", amount: number}`, compute the net balance, find the largest transaction, and group transactions by type using array methods.

explanation:
The DOM (Document Object Model) is the browser's in-memory representation of your HTML. JavaScript selects elements with `querySelector`/`querySelectorAll`, then reads or modifies their properties, attributes, and styles. Every change to the DOM triggers potential reflows and repaints â€” minimizing DOM operations is key to performance.

Events let JavaScript respond to user actions. Events bubble up from the target element to the root â€” this enables event delegation, where you attach one listener to a parent instead of one per child. `addEventListener` is the modern API; inline `onclick` attributes are legacy and limited.
key_takeaway: Use `querySelector` for selection, `addEventListener` for events, and event delegation (`e.target.matches()`) to handle dynamic lists with a single listener.
try_it: Build a dynamic todo list: one input and a button. On click, add the item as an `<li>` with a delete button. Use event delegation on the `<ul>` to handle deletes without per-item listeners.

explanation:
JavaScript is single-threaded â€” it can only do one thing at a time. Async operations (network requests, timers, file reads) use callbacks, but nested callbacks create "callback hell." Promises represent future values that resolve or reject, and `async/await` is syntactic sugar that makes promise-based code read like synchronous code.

The event loop processes the call stack, then microtasks (Promises), then macrotasks (setTimeout, I/O). Understanding this order explains why `Promise.resolve().then(...)` runs before `setTimeout(..., 0)`. Error handling uses `.catch()` for promises or `try/catch` for async/await.
key_takeaway: Use `async/await` for sequential operations, `Promise.all` for parallel operations, and `Promise.allSettled` when you need all results regardless of failures.
try_it: Write an `async function retry(fn, maxAttempts)` that retries a failing async function up to N times with exponential backoff (100ms, 200ms, 400ms...).

explanation:
ES6 (2015) transformed JavaScript from a quirky scripting language into a modern programming language. The key additions â€” `let`/`const`, arrow functions, destructuring, template literals, modules, classes, and Promises â€” are now the baseline for all modern JS development. Subsequent yearly additions added optional chaining, nullish coalescing, and private class fields.

Modules (`import`/`export`) replaced script tags and global variables. Classes provide familiar OOP syntax (though prototypal inheritance underlies them). The spread/rest operators (`...`) handle array/object copying and function arguments elegantly.
key_takeaway: Use `??` instead of `||` for defaults (handles 0 and "" correctly), `?.` for safe property access, and `#` for truly private class fields.
try_it: Create a `Playlist` class with private `#songs` array, public `add(title, artist)`, `remove(title)`, and a `shuffle()` method that randomizes order. Use optional chaining when accessing nested song metadata.

explanation:
JavaScript errors are objects with `name`, `message`, and `stack` properties. The `try/catch/finally` block catches synchronous errors; `.catch()` and `try/catch` with `async/await` handle asynchronous errors. Unhandled promise rejections and uncaught exceptions can crash Node.js or silently fail in browsers.

Debugging is a skill, not a chore. `console.table` formats arrays as tables, `console.time` measures execution, `debugger` pauses execution in DevTools, and the Network tab reveals API failures. The `Error` object's `stack` trace shows the exact call path that led to the error.
key_takeaway: Create custom error classes with error codes for domain-specific failures, use `console.table`/`console.time` for debugging, and always handle promise rejections.
try_it: Write a `safeJsonParse(str)` function that returns `{ success: true, data }` or `{ success: false, error }` without throwing. Test it with valid JSON, invalid JSON, and edge cases like `null`, `undefined`, and empty strings.

explanation:
ES Modules (`import`/`export`) are the standard module system for JavaScript. They're statically analyzable (enabling tree-shaking), support async loading, and have their own scope. CommonJS (`require`/`module.exports`) is Node.js's legacy system. Modern projects use ES Modules everywhere.

Tooling transforms modern JS for browsers. Bundlers (Vite, webpack, esbuild) combine modules into optimized bundles. Transpilers (Babel, SWC) convert modern syntax for older browsers. Linters (ESLint) catch bugs before runtime. Package managers (npm, pnpm, yarn) handle dependencies. Understanding this pipeline is essential for production development.
key_takeaway: ES Modules with `type: "module"` in package.json is the standard â€” use Vite for development, ESLint for linting, and Vitest for testing.
try_it: Create a small project with `utils/math.js` (named exports), `utils/format.js` (default export), and `utils/index.js` (barrel re-exports). Import everything in `main.js` using both named and default imports.

explanation:
JavaScript problems in interviews often test closures, async/await, event handling, and prototype chain understanding. The most common patterns involve array manipulation with higher-order functions (map, filter, reduce), string parsing, and working with objects as hash maps. Understanding the event loop and microtask queue is essential for async problems.

Beyond algorithmic problems, JavaScript interviews frequently ask you to implement built-in methods from scratch: debounce, throttle, Promise.all, deep clone, and EventEmitter. These test your understanding of the language internals rather than pure algorithm skills. Focus on closures, the `this` keyword, and promise chaining patterns.
key_takeaway: Master closures, array higher-order functions, and Map/Set â€” they solve most JavaScript interview problems elegantly.
try_it: Try solving the first Easy problem before looking at any hints

practice_challenges.title: Two Sum
practice_challenges.hint: Use a Map to store seen values and their indices. For each element, check if (target - element) exists in the map.
practice_challenges.description: Given an array of integers and a target value, return indices of two numbers that add up to the target.

practice_challenges.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
practice_challenges.hint: Track the minimum price seen so far. At each step, calculate profit if you sold today and update the max profit.
practice_challenges.description: Given an array of stock prices where index is the day, find the maximum profit from one buy and one sell.

practice_challenges.title: Product of Array Except Self
practice_challenges.hint: Build prefix products (left to right) and suffix products (right to left). The answer at index i is prefix[i] * suffix[i].
practice_challenges.description: Given an integer array, return an array where each element is the product of all other elements. Solve without division in O(n).

practice_challenges.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
practice_challenges.hint: For each index, expand outward checking if characters match. Handle both odd-length and even-length palindromes.
practice_challenges.description: Given a string, return the longest palindromic substring.

practice_challenges.title: Trapping Rain Water
practice_challenges.hint: Water at each position = min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i]. Use two pointers from both ends, tracking leftMax and rightMax.
practice_challenges.description: Given an elevation map where each bar has width 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.
